exercises.name: Barbell Bench Squat
exercises.notes: Step 1: Set a flat bench behind you and load the barbell in a squat rack at about mid-chest height; step under the bar and position it across your upper back/traps (not on the neck).
instructions: Step 1: Set a flat bench behind you and load the barbell in a squat rack at about mid-chest height; step under the bar and position it across your upper back/traps (not on the neck).
Step 2: Grip the bar slightly wider than shoulder width, lift the chest, brace your abs and unrack the bar by standing up, then step back 1–2 foot lengths until the bench is directly behind you.
Step 3: Stand with feet roughly shoulder-width apart and toes pointed slightly outward; maintain a neutral spine and engaged core throughout.
Step 4: Begin the descent by pushing your hips back slightly and bending the knees, keeping weight through the midfoot/heels and knees tracking over your toes.
Step 5: Lower under control until your glutes lightly touch the bench (brief contact only) — do not fully sit or relax on the bench.
Step 6: Drive through the heels and extend the hips and knees to return to standing, exhaling as you rise and keeping the chest up.
Step 7: Step forward to re-rack the bar safely when finished; use a spotter for heavy loads and avoid bouncing off the bench or rounding the lower back.
exercises.description: A bench (box) squat variation performed with a barbell across the upper back where you sit back to a bench to control depth, emphasizing the quadriceps while also engaging the posterior chain and core for stability.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Romanian Deadlift
exercises.notes: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip, feet about hip‑width apart and toes pointing forward.
instructions: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip, feet about hip‑width apart and toes pointing forward.
Set a slight bend in the knees (soft knees) and brace your core to stabilize the spine before initiating the movement.

Hinge at the hips by pushing your hips straight back while keeping the spine neutral and chest slightly lifted; allow the dumbbells to slide down the front of your thighs and shins, staying close to the body.
Lower until you feel a strong stretch in the hamstrings or the dumbbells reach mid‑shin (maintain neutral cervical alignment, do not round the lower back).
Reverse the motion by driving the hips forward, squeezing the glutes and hamstrings to return to standing while keeping the knees softly bent and the weights close to the legs.
Maintain controlled tempo (slow on the descent, controlled drive on the ascent), keep the shoulders down away from the ears, and avoid using the lower back to lift.
exercises.description: A standing hip‑hinge exercise performed with dumbbells that emphasizes eccentric loading of the hamstrings while the hips move back and forth; the movement also engages the glutes and core to maintain a neutral spine. Keep a slight knee bend and the weights close to the legs throughout.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Single Leg Calf Raise
exercises.notes: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides (or one dumbbell in each hand if preferred), feet hip-width apart.
instructions: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides (or one dumbbell in each hand if preferred), feet hip-width apart.

Shift your weight onto one foot and lift the opposite foot slightly off the floor so you are balancing on a single leg (standing position, single-leg). Keep the supporting knee straight but not locked and torso tall.
Brace your core and maintain a neutral spine; if needed lightly touch a wall or hold a sturdy object for balance (wall-assisted) without leaning.

Slowly raise your heel by plantarflexing the ankle of the supporting leg until you are on the ball of your foot/toes, squeezing the calf at the top. Keep shoulders relaxed and dumbbells at your sides.
Pause 1–2 seconds at the top while maintaining balance and control.
Lower your heel slowly back to the starting position until the sole is flat on the floor (or slightly below the step edge if using a raised surface for increased range of motion).
Complete the desired number of repetitions on that leg, then switch to the other leg and repeat.
exercises.description: A single-leg standing calf raise with dumbbells isolates the calf (plantarflexors) on one side while challenging balance and stability. It builds strength and size in the calves while the core, grip, and leg stabilizers assist.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Clean grip Front Squat
exercises.notes: Set up the barbell in a front-rack position using a clean grip: hands just outside shoulders, fingers under the bar, elbows lifted so upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor; stand with feet about shoulder-width apart and toes slightly turned out.
instructions: Set up the barbell in a front-rack position using a clean grip: hands just outside shoulders, fingers under the bar, elbows lifted so upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor; stand with feet about shoulder-width apart and toes slightly turned out.
Brace your core and keep chest up and back straight (neutral spine); eyes forward and shoulders high to support the bar on the front deltoids/upper chest.
Inhale, initiate the movement by bending at the hips and knees simultaneously, pushing the knees outward to track over the toes and keeping the elbows up so the bar does not roll forward.
Descend under control until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor (or to a comfortable depth while maintaining an upright torso), keeping weight distributed through the midfoot and heels.
Pause briefly at the bottom while maintaining core tension and a proud chest, then drive the feet into the ground (focus on heels) to extend the knees and hips and return to standing.
Exhale as you stand up, fully extending hips and knees while keeping elbows high and the bar stable on your shoulders; reset breathing and posture before the next rep.

Keep repetitions controlled and avoid letting the torso collapse forward, the elbows drop, or the knees cave inward; if wrist discomfort occurs with the clean grip, adjust hand position or use cross-arm/front-rack variation.
exercises.description: A front-loaded barbell squat performed with a clean grip (bar rests on the front shoulders, elbows high) that emphasizes knee extension to target the quadriceps while also engaging the posterior chain and core for stability. Maintain an upright torso and high elbows throughout the movement.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Stiff Leg Deadlift
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart, dumbbells held in front of thighs with a neutral grip; keep a slight, fixed bend in the knees (stiff‑leg position).
instructions: Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart, dumbbells held in front of thighs with a neutral grip; keep a slight, fixed bend in the knees (stiff‑leg position).
Brace your core and retract the shoulders slightly to maintain a neutral spine throughout the movement.
Hinge at the hips by pushing them straight back while keeping the dumbbells close to your legs; allow the torso to come forward until you feel a stretch in the hamstrings (usually to mid‑shin).
Keep the back flat (avoid rounding) and maintain the slight knee bend; eyes should be looking a few feet ahead to help keep the spine neutral.
Pause briefly at the bottom when you feel a firm hamstring stretch, then drive the hips forward by contracting the hamstrings and glutes to return to standing.
Squeeze the glutes at the top and avoid leaning back or hyperextending the spine.
Breathe in while lowering, exhale as you drive the hips forward; maintain controlled tempo and a firm grip (forearms engaged) throughout.
exercises.description: A hinge-style deadlift performed with minimal knee bend to load the posterior chain, primarily targeting the hamstrings while also engaging the glutes and lower back. Dumbbells are lowered along the legs by driving the hips back and then the hips are thrust forward to return to standing.

exercises.name: Lever Belt Sumo Squat
exercises.notes: Stand on the platform facing the machine with the lever belt secured low on your hips; grab the front handles for light support if available.
instructions: Stand on the platform facing the machine with the lever belt secured low on your hips; grab the front handles for light support if available.
Position your feet much wider than shoulder-width with toes pointed out at ~30–45 degrees (sumo stance).
Brace your core, keep chest tall and a neutral spine, and allow a slight forward torso lean while maintaining an upright upper body.

Begin the movement by pushing your hips back and bending at the knees, lowering yourself into a squat until your thighs are roughly parallel to the platform or a comfortable depth where knees track over toes.
Keep your knees tracking in line with your toes and distribute weight through your heels and mid-foot; avoid collapsing the knees inward.
Drive forcefully through the heels and hips to return to standing, fully extending the hips at the top while resisting the lever’s load throughout the motion.
Control the descent on each rep, breathe in on the way down and exhale as you drive up, and perform repetitions with a steady tempo.
exercises.description: A wide-stance (sumo) squat performed with a lever belt/harness attached to a machine; emphasizes hip drive and inner-thigh engagement while reducing load on the spine. Use a wide, turned-out foot position and sit back between the heels, then drive up through the hips.

exercises.name: Barbell Lunge
exercises.notes: Position a loaded barbell in a rack at upper‑chest height, step under it and place it across your upper back/traps, hands slightly wider than shoulder width with a firm overhand grip; unrack the bar and take one or two steps back to a stable, shoulder‑width starting stance (standing).
instructions: Position a loaded barbell in a rack at upper‑chest height, step under it and place it across your upper back/traps, hands slightly wider than shoulder width with a firm overhand grip; unrack the bar and take one or two steps back to a stable, shoulder‑width starting stance (standing).
Brace your abs and keep a neutral spine and chest up; feet should be hip to shoulder width for balance and toes pointing forward.
Take a controlled long step forward with one foot (forward lunge), planting the front foot flat while keeping weight centered over the midfoot to heel.

Lower your body by bending both knees until the front thigh is about parallel to the floor and the rear knee is close to the ground (approximately 90° at the front knee); keep the front knee tracking over the toes and avoid excessive forward collapse of the torso.
Pause briefly at the bottom, then drive through the front heel to extend the front knee and hip and return to the starting standing position.
Repeat for the desired reps, alternating legs each repetition or complete all reps on one side before switching; breathe in while lowering and exhale while driving up.
Maintain controlled tempo, core bracing, and even foot placement each rep; use a power rack or spotter for safety when loading heavy.
exercises.description: A weighted forward lunge performed with a barbell resting on the upper back that emphasizes single‑leg knee extension to work the front thigh while also engaging the hips and core for stability. Each repetition involves stepping forward, lowering until the front thigh is near parallel to the floor, then driving back to standing.

exercises.name: Barbell Romanian Deadlift
exercises.notes: Stand upright holding a barbell with an overhand (or mixed) grip, feet about hip-width apart and the bar resting against your thighs; maintain a neutral spine and engaged core.
instructions: Stand upright holding a barbell with an overhand (or mixed) grip, feet about hip-width apart and the bar resting against your thighs; maintain a neutral spine and engaged core.
Set a small, consistent bend in the knees (soft knees) — do not bend into a squat; this is a hinge at the hips, not a knee-dominant lift.
Take a breath in, brace your core, then push your hips back while keeping the chest up and spine neutral; allow the bar to travel down close to the front of your legs.
Lower the bar until you feel a strong but safe stretch in the hamstrings (typically to mid‑shin), keeping the bar close to the body and shoulders pulled back — avoid rounding the lower back.
Pause briefly at the bottom while maintaining tension in the hamstrings and back; keep your head in a neutral alignment (looking a few feet in front of you).
Exhale and drive your hips forward by contracting the glutes and hamstrings to return to the standing start position; do not hyperextend the lower back at the top.
Control the descent and ascent on every rep, using a weight that lets you maintain a neutral spine and full hip hinge pattern.
If grip is limiting, switch to a mixed grip or use straps; always prioritize form over load and stop if you feel lumbar rounding or sharp pain.
exercises.description: A bilateral hip-hinge performed from standing that emphasizes the hamstrings by lowering a loaded barbell along the thighs with a neutral spine and slight knee bend; returned to standing by driving the hips forward and squeezing the glutes. Builds posterior chain strength and improves hip extension.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Calf Press
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat and back pad so your knees are roughly 90 degrees and your hips are supported while seated (seated position).
instructions: Adjust the seat and back pad so your knees are roughly 90 degrees and your hips are supported while seated (seated position).
Select an appropriate weight and ensure the machine's safety catch or lever is engaged before sitting.
Place the balls of both feet shoulder-width on the footplate with toes pointing forward; heels should hang slightly to allow a full range of motion.
Grip the side handles or seat handles to stabilize the torso and unlock the safety if applicable.
Exhale and press through the balls of your feet, driving the footplate away by extending your ankles into full plantarflexion (raise your heels as high as possible).
Pause briefly at the top for a strong contraction in the calves, then inhale and slowly lower your heels back down until you feel a stretch in the calves (controlled eccentric phase).
Repeat for desired reps. After finishing, lock the safety catch before removing your feet from the footplate.
exercises.description: Seated calf press performed on a lever machine targets the calf muscles by plantarflexing the ankle against resistance while seated with knees bent. It isolates the gastrocnemius/soleus complex for focused calf development.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Low Bar Squat
exercises.notes: Set the bar in a squat rack at mid-back height.
instructions: Set the bar in a squat rack at mid-back height. Step under the bar and place it across the rear delts/upper trapezius area just below the top of the shoulders (low-bar position), not on the neck.
Grip the bar slightly wider than shoulder width, retract the shoulder blades and squeeze the upper back to create a stable shelf; elbows should be pointed slightly down and back to support the bar.
Unrack the bar by straightening the legs and take 1–2 small steps back to clear the rack. Stand with feet about shoulder-width apart or slightly wider, toes pointed slightly outward.
Brace your core (tighten abs and breathe into the belly) and maintain a neutral spine; keep chest up and eyes forward. The torso will lean forward more than a high-bar squat but should remain rigid.
Begin the descent by pushing the hips back and bending the knees simultaneously; keep the knees tracking over the toes and push them outward to avoid caving in.
Lower until the thighs are at least parallel to the floor (or to your comfortable depth while maintaining form), keeping weight distributed through the midfoot and heels.
Drive upward by pushing through the heels and extending the hips and knees simultaneously while maintaining a braced core and tight upper back; exhale as you rise.
Return to a fully upright standing position with hips locked out, then repeat for the desired reps. Control the bar on each rep and reset your breath and brace between reps if needed.
When finished, walk the bar forward and carefully re-rack it on the squat rack. Use a spotter or safety pins if lifting heavy or training near failure.
exercises.description: A loaded barbell squat with the bar positioned low on the rear shoulders (across the posterior deltoids), emphasizing the thighs and posterior chain. The movement consists of a controlled hip-back-and-down descent to roughly parallel followed by an explosive drive back to standing.

exercises.name: Barbell Dimel Deadlift
exercises.notes: Slightly bend the knees (soft knee) and brace your core, maintaining a neutral spine and chest up throughout the movement.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; grip the bar with both hands just outside the legs using an overhand or mixed grip.
Slightly bend the knees (soft knee) and brace your core, maintaining a neutral spine and chest up throughout the movement.
Initiate the movement by pushing the hips back (hip hinge) and lower the barbell down the front of your legs toward mid-shin while keeping the bar close to your body.
Keep the neck neutral, shoulders retracted, and do not let the lower back round as you descend; feel a stretch in the hamstrings.
Reverse the motion by driving the hips forward explosively while pulling the bar up along the thighs to a near-lockout (stand tall), squeezing the glutes at the top.
Control the descent and repeat for the desired reps, breathing in on the lower and exhaling as you drive the hips forward.
Use lighter weight and higher reps if performing Dimel-style sets, and stop or reduce range if you feel lower-back rounding or pain.
exercises.description: A barbell hip-hinge exercise performed from a standing position that emphasizes eccentric and concentric work of the hamstrings by lowering the bar toward the shins and driving the hips forward to stand. The movement trains the posterior chain with a short range of motion and controlled tempo.

exercises.name: Lever Standing Rear Kick
exercises.notes: Set the lever or ankle attachment to the appropriate height and select a manageable resistance; stand facing the machine and grip the front handles for stability.
instructions: Set the lever or ankle attachment to the appropriate height and select a manageable resistance; stand facing the machine and grip the front handles for stability.

Position the working leg so the cuff or pad is secured at the back of the thigh or ankle (depending on machine); stand upright on the support leg with a slight bend in the knee and feet hip-width apart.
Brace your core (abs engaged), keep the torso upright with a small forward lean if needed for balance, and maintain a neutral spine throughout.
Drive the working leg straight back by extending at the hip (avoid excessive lumbar arching), squeezing the glutes at the top of the movement; keep the knee slightly bent and the foot neutral.
Pause briefly at full hip extension, then return the leg under control to the starting position without letting the weight stack slam.
Repeat for the prescribed reps, then switch sides. Breathe out on the kickback (concentric) and inhale as you return (eccentric).
exercises.description: A standing hip extension performed on a lever/cable machine where one leg drives backward against resistance to isolate and strengthen the glute muscles. The exercise is executed while standing and holding the machine for balance, focusing on controlled hip extension and glute contraction.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Calf Press
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat and foot platform so your knees are roughly at 90 degrees when your feet are on the plate and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Adjust the seat and foot platform so your knees are roughly at 90 degrees when your feet are on the plate and select an appropriate weight.
Sit with your back fully supported against the pad and both feet placed hip-width on the lower edge of the foot platform (balls of feet and toes on the platform, heels free to move).
Grip the handles or seat sides for stability and ensure the safety latch or stops are set before starting.
Unlatch the machine (if applicable) and begin from a neutral/dorsiflexed position with a slight stretch in the calves.
Exhale and press through the balls of your feet by plantarflexing the ankles to raise the platform until your calves are fully contracted; do not use the knees or hips to drive the movement.
Pause briefly at the top and squeeze the calves, then inhale and slowly lower the platform under control until you feel a stretch in the calves (avoid dropping quickly or bouncing).
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, then re-engage the safety latch before exiting the machine.
exercises.description: Seated on a lever calf press machine, this exercise uses controlled ankle plantarflexion to raise the foot platform and load the calf muscles. It emphasizes the gastrocnemius/soleus complex while the torso and knees remain supported.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Leg Raise Crunch (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat and back pad so your lower back is supported and the roller pad sits just above your ankles when knees are bent.
instructions: Adjust the seat and back pad so your lower back is supported and the roller pad sits just above your ankles when knees are bent.
Load the desired plate weight and sit with your back against the back pad, knees bent and feet secured under the roller pad; grasp the machine handles near your head to stabilize the upper body.
Start with torso upright and knees pulled slightly toward the torso (neutral tension) and inhale.
Exhale and simultaneously curl your spine forward and lift your knees slightly toward your chest by contracting your abs, performing a controlled crunch + leg raise against the plate resistance.
Pause briefly at the top while keeping the contraction in your abs and avoiding excessive neck strain or momentum.
Inhale and slowly return to the start position, extending the spine and lowering the knees under control until tension is maintained.
Repeat for the prescribed number of repetitions, keeping movements slow and controlled and avoiding excessive lumbar rounding or swinging.
exercises.description: A plate-loaded seated crunch that combines a leg raise with a spinal crunch to emphasize the rectus abdominis. Performed seated with feet hooked under a roller pad while curling the torso and hips forward against resistance.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Calf Raise (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Place the balls of your feet on the edge of the foot platform with heels hanging freely off the platform.
instructions: Sit on the seated calf raise machine so your knees are bent at about 90 degrees and your back is upright (seated position).
Place the balls of your feet on the edge of the foot platform with heels hanging freely off the platform.
Position the padded lever across your thighs just above the knees and load the desired plates on the lever (plate-loaded).
Grasp the handles or machine grips for stability and brace your core.
Begin with your heels lowered until you feel a stretch in the calves (ankle dorsiflexion).
Drive through the balls of your feet to raise your heels as high as possible (ankle plantarflexion), lifting the lever and plates.
Pause and squeeze your calves at the top for 1–2 seconds while keeping knees bent and torso stable.
Lower your heels slowly and under control back to the starting stretched position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, avoiding bouncing or using hip/knee extension to assist the movement.
exercises.description: Seated, plate-loaded calf raise that isolates the calf musculature by plantarflexing the ankles while the knees are bent; the lever pad rests on the thighs and weight is applied via a plate-loaded lever. Performed from a seated position to emphasize the soleus and overall calf development.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Side Bend
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart and knees slightly soft; hold one dumbbell in the hand on the working side with the arm extended down by your thigh.
instructions: Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart and knees slightly soft; hold one dumbbell in the hand on the working side with the arm extended down by your thigh.
Place the non-working hand lightly behind your head or across your chest to avoid pulling on the neck; keep your chest up and shoulders back (standing position).
Keep your hips and pelvis fixed — do not shift them laterally — and maintain a neutral spine throughout the movement.
Slowly bend your torso directly to the side of the dumbbell (lateral flexion) by contracting the obliques; lead with the ribcage so the movement comes from the torso, not the hips.
Pause briefly at the bottom of the range without rotating or leaning forward/backward, then contract the abs to return the torso to the starting upright position in a controlled manner.
Breathe in as you lower the torso and exhale as you return upright; perform the desired number of reps then switch sides.
Use a weight that allows smooth, controlled repetitions and avoid jerking or using momentum to pull the torso — stop if you feel low-back pain.
exercises.description: Standing lateral trunk flexion performed while holding a dumbbell at one side to load the obliques. The movement bends the torso toward the weight and then returns upright to work the abs (obliques) with grip and spinal stabilization assistance.

exercises.name: Cable Twist
exercises.notes: Setup: Attach a single handle to a cable pulley positioned at about chest height.
instructions: Setup: Attach a single handle to a cable pulley positioned at about chest height. Stand upright facing the pulley with feet shoulder-width apart or in a slightly staggered stance for balance.
Grip & posture: Grasp the handle with both hands out in front of you, arms extended but not locked, shoulders down and back, knees slightly bent, and spine neutral.
Start position: Take a small step away from the machine so the cable is taut and the weight stack is lifted slightly; keep hips facing forward (minimize hip rotation).
Execute rotation: Exhale and rotate your torso away from the machine by driving the ribcage and shoulders across your hips, keeping your arms extended and the handle at roughly chest height.
Control the return: Inhale and slowly return to the start position under control, resisting the pull of the cable. Do not let the cable snap you back.
Repetitions & tempo: Perform the desired number of controlled repetitions on one side, then switch sides by repositioning so you rotate in the opposite direction.
Form tips: Keep the movement driven by the core (abs) not by the arms; maintain a neutral spine and avoid excessive lumbar extension or leaning. Adjust foot stance for stability as needed.
exercises.description: A standing cable rotation where you hold a single handle with both hands and rotate the torso away from the machine, using resisted trunk rotation to train the abs and improve core rotational strength.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows and thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Retract and depress the shoulder blades (pinch the shoulder blades together) to create a stable chest platform.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over the chest so it is balanced and steady.
Inhale, lower the bar under control to the mid-chest (sternum) keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest while maintaining tightness through the core and legs.
Exhale and drive the bar upward by extending the elbows, pressing the bar back to the start position while keeping wrists neutral.
Avoid excessive elbow flare or bouncing the bar off the chest; maintain a controlled tempo and keep feet planted.
After the set, carefully re-rack the bar by guiding it back onto the bench supports before releasing your grip.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a supine pressing exercise performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also working the triceps and anterior delts. The lifter lowers a barbell to the chest and presses it back to full arm extension under control.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.

Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.

Inhale, then slowly lower the dumbbells in a controlled manner until your elbows are roughly level with the bench or just below chest level, keeping wrists neutral and elbows tracking slightly tucked.

Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulder blade position, then exhale and press the dumbbells upward and slightly together until your arms are almost fully extended — avoid locking the elbows.
Control the descent back to the starting position and repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
To finish the set, bring the dumbbells safely to your knees, sit up, and set them down or have a spotter assist if using heavy weight.

Dumbbells allow a greater range of motion and require more stabilization from the forearms and serratus anterior.

exercises.name: Barbell Clean and Press
exercises.notes: Step 1: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, toes slightly turned out, barbell over mid-foot; grip the bar just outside the knees with a full overhand or mixed grip and keep the chest up and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Step 1: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, toes slightly turned out, barbell over mid-foot; grip the bar just outside the knees with a full overhand or mixed grip and keep the chest up and core braced (standing position).
Step 2: Initiate the lift with a hip hinge by bending at the hips and knees keeping the spine neutral and shoulders slightly in front of the bar; maintain a tight core and engaged lats.

Step 3: Drive through the heels and extend the hips, knees and ankles explosively (triple extension) to accelerate the bar upward while shrugging the shoulders and pulling the elbows high and out (clean pull).

Step 4: As the bar reaches peak upward momentum, quickly drop under the bar into a partial front squat and rotate the elbows forward to receive the bar on the front of the shoulders in a front-rack position (clean, front-rack).

Step 5: Stand tall from the front-rack by extending the hips and knees to full standing, reset your core and grip, then press the bar overhead by extending the elbows and driving the head slightly forward until arms are fully locked out (press).
Step 6: Lower the bar under control back to the front-rack position, then carefully return the bar to the floor with a hip hinge and bend the knees to reset for the next repetition.

Step 7: Maintain braced abs and a neutral spine throughout, breathe consistently (exhale during the press), and use controlled tempo—perform the clean explosively but receive and press with stability and control.
exercises.description: A compound Olympic-style lift combining a barbell clean (lifting the bar to a front-rack position) followed immediately by a press overhead, training the shoulders along with legs, hips and upper back. It develops explosive power, pressing strength, and full-body coordination.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Front Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back firmly supported and feet flat on the floor.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back firmly supported and feet flat on the floor.
Pick up a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral or overhand grip and let your arms hang straight down at your sides, wrists neutral and a slight bend in the elbows.

Brace your core, keep your chest up and neck neutral, and exhale as you raise both dumbbells together in front of you in a controlled arc to about shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Keep the elbows slightly bent and wrists firm; avoid swinging or using momentum from the torso or legs.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders engaged, then inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, choosing a weight that allows strict form; stop if you feel shoulder joint pain.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench, both dumbbells are raised straight forward to shoulder height to emphasize the anterior portion of the delts. The incline and controlled motion reduce momentum and isolate the front shoulder muscles.

exercises.name: Cable Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles. Stand centered between the two towers and grasp each handle with the opposite hand so the cables cross in front of you (or hold same-side handles if preferred).
Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (neutral spine). Keep a slight bend in the elbows and wrists neutral.
Start with the handles low by your thighs; palms can face inward or slightly forward. Maintain a small forward lean from the hips if comfortable to allow a natural arc.
Keeping the elbows slightly bent, exhale and raise both arms out to the sides in a smooth arc until your hands reach approximately shoulder height. Lead the movement with the elbow, not the wrist.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts; avoid shrugging the shoulders upward toward your ears.
Inhale and lower the handles slowly and under control back to the starting position, keeping tension on the cables throughout the descent.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. Use a lighter weight if you cannot maintain strict form or if you begin using torso momentum.
exercises.description: A standing cable lateral raise performed from low pulleys to isolate the lateral head of the delts; cables provide constant tension while you lift the arms out to the sides to shoulder height. The movement is done with a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to minimize momentum.

Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place a pair of dumbbells nearby. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place a pair of dumbbells nearby.
Sit on the bench with the dumbbells resting on your thighs, feet flat on the floor for stability.
Use your legs to help lift the dumbbells as you lie back so the weights are beside your upper chest; hold them with a neutral or slightly supinated grip.
Retract your shoulder blades and press your upper back into the bench so your chest is proud (maintain a stable supine position on the incline).
Plant your feet and brace your core to avoid excessive arching of the spine.
Press the dumbbells upward and slightly together in a controlled path until your arms are almost fully extended (do not lock the elbows).
Exhale as you press up, pause briefly at the top with control, then inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the start position at the upper chest level.
Keep the elbows at a moderate angle (not flared excessively) and the wrists neutral throughout the movement.
Repeat for desired repetitions and use a spotter for heavy sets or if you cannot safely return the weights to your thighs/stand them up.
exercises.description: The dumbbell incline bench press is performed lying supine on an incline bench and pressing dumbbells upward to emphasize the upper portion of the chest. It builds strength and mass in the upper pectorals while also recruiting the front delts and triceps.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Upright Row
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart, knees soft and core braced (neutral spine). Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart, knees soft and core braced (neutral spine).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with an overhand (pronated) grip, arms extended down in front of you and weights close to your thighs.
Initiate the movement by driving your elbows upward and outward, keeping the dumbbells close to the body and leading with the elbows.
Raise the dumbbells until your elbows reach roughly chest/shoulder height or just below chin level, keeping wrists relatively neutral and palms facing your body or slightly inward.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders engaged but avoid excessive shrugging or leaning back.
Lower the dumbbells slowly and under control back to the starting position, fully extending the arms without locking the elbows.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions while maintaining an upright standing position, steady breathing, and avoiding momentum or swinging.
exercises.description: A standing dumbbell upright row where you pull the weights vertically toward your upper chest, emphasizing the shoulder (deltoid) muscles with assistance from the trapezius, biceps, and forearms. Perform with a controlled motion and elbows leading to keep tension on the delts.

exercises.name: Cable Seated Face Pull (with rope)
exercises.notes: Set a rope handle on a high pulley (about head/eye level) and select an appropriate weight. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set a rope handle on a high pulley (about head/eye level) and select an appropriate weight.
Sit upright facing the machine with feet flat on the floor and knees bent (or seated on a bench); keep a tall spine and neutral head position.
Grasp the rope with a neutral grip (thumbs pointing toward you) and extend your arms so there is tension on the cable.
Initiate the movement by pulling the rope toward your face, leading with your elbows and keeping them at about shoulder height.
As you pull, externally rotate your shoulders so the ends of the rope separate and your hands move either side of your face; squeeze your shoulder blades together at the end range.
Pause briefly at peak contraction, keeping the torso stable and avoiding excessive backward lean or shrugging.
Slowly return the rope to the start position with control, maintaining tension in the cable and scapular control throughout.
Repeat for the desired number of reps and maintain controlled tempo to protect the shoulder joint.
exercises.description: A seated cable face pull using a rope attachment that emphasizes the rear delts and upper posterior shoulder musculature by pulling the rope toward the face while externally rotating the hands and squeezing the shoulder blades.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

exercises.name: Lever Chest Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on the machine with your back and head pressed firmly against the backpad and both feet flat on the floor. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit upright on the machine with your back and head pressed firmly against the backpad and both feet flat on the floor.
Adjust the seat height so the handles are at mid-chest level and your elbows start roughly at or slightly below shoulder height.
Grasp the handles with a neutral or overhand grip, keep your shoulder blades gently retracted and your chest lifted.
Inhale, then press the handles forward and slightly together in a controlled motion until your arms are almost fully extended—do not lock your elbows.
Exhale as you push and focus on contracting the pectorals; keep the motion steady and avoid using momentum.
Pause briefly at the end of the press, then slowly return the handles to the start position under control, allowing the chest to stretch but keeping the shoulder blades stable.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, maintaining a steady tempo and ensuring the back and head remain against the pad throughout.
exercises.description: Seated lever chest press performed on a plate-loaded or selectorized lever machine that drives horizontal pressing to work the chest. The movement presses the handles forward from chest level to full arm extension, emphasizing the pectoral muscles while the delts and triceps assist.

exercises.name: Cable Standing Row
exercises.notes: Set the cable pulley to about chest height and attach a close-grip or double-handle. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set the cable pulley to about chest height and attach a close-grip or double-handle.
Stand facing the machine with feet hip-width apart (or a slight staggered stance for balance) and a soft bend in the knees; grasp the handle with a neutral or overhand grip.
Step back until the cable is taut and hinge slightly at the hips so your torso is upright but tilted forward a few degrees; brace your core and keep a neutral spine.
Begin the movement by pulling your shoulder blades together (scapular retraction), then drive your elbows straight back toward your sides, keeping them close to the body.
Pull until your hands reach your lower chest/upper abdomen and you feel a full contraction in the lats and upper back; avoid excessive torso lean or momentum.
Pause 0–1 seconds at the top while squeezing the shoulder blades, then slowly extend your arms back to the start under control, maintaining core tension.
Repeat for the desired reps and adjust weight so you can maintain strict form for the full set.
exercises.description: A standing cable rowing movement that targets the lats and mid/upper back by pulling a cable handle toward the torso while maintaining a braced, upright stance. Emphasizes scapular retraction and controlled concentric and eccentric phases.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms fully extended down at your sides and palms facing inward; keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and neck neutral, and avoid arching your lower back.
Exhale and lift both dumbbells upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your hands reach about shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top, maintaining tension in the delts and avoiding momentum or swinging.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting hanging position under control.
Perform the desired repetitions, keeping movement smooth and avoiding use of torso momentum; reduce weight if you need to swing to complete reps.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench, this variation of the dumbbell raise emphasizes the front portion of the shoulder (anterior delts) by lifting the weights from a hanging position up to about shoulder height. The incline angle shifts some load onto the chest (pectorals) while isolating the delts and minimizing torso swing.

exercises.name: Cable Seated Rear Delt Raise
exercises.notes: Set a single handle or dual handles on the low pulley(s) and select a light to moderate weight. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set a single handle or dual handles on the low pulley(s) and select a light to moderate weight.
Sit on the bench facing the pulley, feet flat on the floor and knees slightly bent.
Hinge forward at the hips so your torso is leaning forward (seated, leaning forward position) with a neutral spine and core braced; let your arms hang down toward the pulley.
Grasp the handle(s) with a neutral or thumbs-up grip, elbows slightly bent throughout the movement.

Keeping the chest down and shoulder blades relaxed, raise your upper arms out to the side/diagonal (horizontal abduction) until the elbows reach about shoulder height or just above, leading with the elbows.
Pause briefly at the top and squeeze the rear delts and upper back; avoid shrugging the shoulders toward the ears.
Slowly lower the handle(s) back to the start under control until your arms are fully extended and tension is maintained.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining a steady tempo, controlled motion, and neutral spine throughout.
exercises.description: A seated cable variation of the rear delt raise that isolates the posterior deltoid by performing a horizontal abduction/flare of the upper arm while leaning forward. Performed with a low pulley to keep constant tension through the movement.

exercises.name: Cable Seated Face Pull (with rope)
exercises.notes: Set a rope handle on a high pulley (about head/eye level) and select an appropriate weight. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set a rope handle on a high pulley (about head/eye level) and select an appropriate weight.
Sit upright facing the machine with feet flat on the floor and knees bent (or seated on a bench); keep a tall spine and neutral head position.
Grasp the rope with a neutral grip (thumbs pointing toward you) and extend your arms so there is tension on the cable.
Initiate the movement by pulling the rope toward your face, leading with your elbows and keeping them at about shoulder height.
As you pull, externally rotate your shoulders so the ends of the rope separate and your hands move either side of your face; squeeze your shoulder blades together at the end range.
Pause briefly at peak contraction, keeping the torso stable and avoiding excessive backward lean or shrugging.
Slowly return the rope to the start position with control, maintaining tension in the cable and scapular control throughout.
Repeat for the desired number of reps and maintain controlled tempo to protect the shoulder joint.
exercises.description: A seated cable face pull using a rope attachment that emphasizes the rear delts and upper posterior shoulder musculature by pulling the rope toward the face while externally rotating the hands and squeezing the shoulder blades.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over the midfoot; grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width using an overhand or mixed grip.
Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly to set the upper back before the first rep.
Pull the barbell upward toward the lower chest/upper abdomen by driving the elbows back and keeping them close to the body; focus on squeezing the upper back at the top of the movement.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining a flat back, then lower the barbell under control until arms are nearly extended while preserving the hip-hinge position.
Breathe out while pulling the bar up and inhale while lowering it; keep the torso stable and avoid rounding the lower back or using excessive torso momentum.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, and reset your hip-hinge position between sets if you lose posture.
exercises.description: A hip-hinged barbell row that trains the upper back by pulling the loaded barbell toward the torso while maintaining a neutral spine. It also recruits the lats, arms and posterior chain for stability and power.

exercises.name: Barbell Paused Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (or at a comfortable angled bend); keep the back flat and head in neutral alignment.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (or at a comfortable angled bend); keep the back flat and head in neutral alignment.
Grip the bar with a shoulder-width overhand grip (or mixed grip if needed for heavy loads) and brace your core.
Initiate the movement by pulling your shoulder blades together and driving your elbows back, bringing the bar toward your lower chest/upper abdomen.
Hold and squeeze the shoulder blades together at the top for 1–2 seconds (the paused portion) while keeping the torso stable and avoiding excess torso rotation.
Lower the bar under control until arms are extended and the bar is near knee level or a natural hang, maintaining a neutral spine throughout.
Repeat for desired reps while breathing out during the pull and inhaling as you lower; avoid rounding the back or using excessive momentum.
exercises.description: A bent-over barbell row performed from a hip-hinged position with a brief pause at the top of the pull to maximize scapular retraction and time under tension. Emphasizes rowing the elbows back while keeping the torso stable and the spine neutral.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Sumo Deadlift High Pull
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips and bend the knees to lower into a deep squat (sumo position) until your hips are below or near knee level; keep chest up and maintain a neutral spine.
instructions: Stand with a wide (sumo) stance: feet wider than shoulder-width, toes turned slightly outward and bar over the midfoot.
Hinge at the hips and bend the knees to lower into a deep squat (sumo position) until your hips are below or near knee level; keep chest up and maintain a neutral spine.
Grip the bar with both hands inside your knees (narrow grip) and engage your core; shoulders should be slightly in front of the bar.
Take a full breath and brace, then initiate the movement by driving through the heels and extending the hips and knees explosively.

Keep the bar close to your body as you extend; as the bar passes the knees, perform an explosive shrug and pull with the elbows driving upward and outward to bring the bar toward chest/chin height (high pull).
Aim to lead with the elbows so the forearms and delts receive the pull; fully extend hips at the top and stand tall without hyperextending the lower back.
Lower the bar under control by reversing the motion: allow the hips to hinge, knees to bend, and guide the bar back to the floor while maintaining a neutral spine.
Reset stance and breathing between reps; inhale and brace before each lift and exhale at/after the top. Maintain knee tracking over toes and avoid rounding the back.
exercises.description: A compound, explosive lift performed from a wide (sumo) stance that combines a deadlift hip/leg drive with an upright high pull to bring the bar toward the chest. It trains lower-body extension and upper-body pulling (shoulder shrug and elbow lift) in a single fluid movement.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over the midfoot; grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width using an overhand or mixed grip.
Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly to set the upper back before the first rep.
Pull the barbell upward toward the lower chest/upper abdomen by driving the elbows back and keeping them close to the body; focus on squeezing the upper back at the top of the movement.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining a flat back, then lower the barbell under control until arms are nearly extended while preserving the hip-hinge position.
Breathe out while pulling the bar up and inhale while lowering it; keep the torso stable and avoid rounding the lower back or using excessive torso momentum.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, and reset your hip-hinge position between sets if you lose posture.
exercises.description: A hip-hinged barbell row that trains the upper back by pulling the loaded barbell toward the torso while maintaining a neutral spine. It also recruits the lats, arms and posterior chain for stability and power.

exercises.name: Chest Dip (on dip pull-up cage)
exercises.notes: Grip the parallel bars of the dip/pull-up cage with hands shoulder-width or slightly wider and arms fully extended; support your body weight with feet off the floor (knees can be bent or ankles crossed).
instructions: Grip the parallel bars of the dip/pull-up cage with hands shoulder-width or slightly wider and arms fully extended; support your body weight with feet off the floor (knees can be bent or ankles crossed).
Lean your torso forward ~20–30 degrees from vertical and retract your shoulder blades slightly to place emphasis on the pectorals; keep a neutral neck and core braced.
Inhale and slowly lower your body by bending the elbows and allowing them to flare mildly outward, keeping the forward lean so your chest moves toward the bars.
Descend until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor or your chest reaches the level of the bars (stop earlier if you feel shoulder pain); maintain control—do not drop suddenly.
Exhale and press through your palms to straighten the elbows and return to the start position with arms extended, keeping the forward lean and core engaged.
Perform reps with controlled tempo (e.g., 2–3 seconds down, 1 second pause, explosive up); avoid locking shoulders at the top and do not hyperextend the elbows.
If the movement is too difficult, use an assisted dip machine, resistance band support, or place feet on a low platform until you can perform unassisted reps with proper form.
exercises.description: A chest-focused dip performed on parallel bars/dip station with a forward lean to emphasize the pectorals while still engaging the triceps and delts. Keep legs off the floor and descend in a controlled arc until chest is level with the hands, then press back up.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Landmine Squat and Press
exercises.notes: Set up the landmine by securing one end of the barbell in the pivot and load the free end with appropriate weight; stand facing the bar with feet about shoulder‑width apart and toes slightly turned out.
instructions: Set up the landmine by securing one end of the barbell in the pivot and load the free end with appropriate weight; stand facing the bar with feet about shoulder‑width apart and toes slightly turned out.
Grip the end of the bar with both hands and hold it close to your chest in a front‑rack/goblet position (elbows tucked under the bar, forearms vertical) while standing tall and bracing your abs.
Begin the movement by pushing your hips back slightly and bending at the knees to lower into a controlled squat until your thighs are roughly parallel to the floor or a comfortable depth is reached.

Drive through your heels and midfoot to stand up explosively; as you reach near full hip and knee extension, press the bar upward and slightly forward using your delts and triceps until your arms are extended.
Pause briefly at the top with a stable torso, then slowly lower the bar back to the chest/front‑rack position under control and immediately move into the next squat.
Maintain a neutral spine, keep the chest up, breathe in before the descent and exhale during the press, and avoid letting the knees cave inwards throughout the movement.
exercises.description: A front‑loaded compound movement performed with a landmine bar that combines a squat with an overhead/forward press, loading the quads during the descent and the shoulders/triceps during the press. It trains lower‑body strength and upper‑body pressing in a single fluid motion.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bent Over Single Arm Row
exercises.notes: Keep your spine neutral, neck in line with the spine, and the non‑working hand resting on your thigh or a bench for support if needed.
instructions: Stand with feet hip‑width apart holding a dumbbell in one hand; bend the knees slightly and hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (bent‑over position).
Keep your spine neutral, neck in line with the spine, and the non‑working hand resting on your thigh or a bench for support if needed.
Let the working arm hang straight down from the shoulder with the palm facing in (neutral grip).
Brace your core and pull the dumbbell straight up toward your hip/waist by driving the elbow back and squeezing the shoulder blade toward the spine; keep the elbow close to the body.
Pause briefly at the top with the scapula retracted, then lower the weight under control until the arm is fully extended.
Repeat for the desired reps, then switch sides. Maintain the hip hinge and avoid rotating the torso or using momentum to lift the weight.
exercises.description: A single‑arm bent‑over row is a standing hip‑hinge rowing movement that pulls a dumbbell toward the hip to develop the lats and upper back while also working the biceps and forearms. It is performed from a bent‑over position with a neutral spine and controlled eccentric lowering.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Hammer Curl to Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart, knees soft, core braced and a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing each other) grip at your sides.
instructions: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart, knees soft, core braced and a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing each other) grip at your sides.
Keep your elbows close to your torso and curl the dumbbells upward in a controlled hammer curl until the dumbbells reach shoulder level.
At the top of the curl, pause briefly; if comfortable, rotate the wrists so the palms face forward or maintain a neutral grip depending on mobility.
Press the dumbbells overhead by extending the elbows and driving through the shoulders until arms are fully extended but not locked, exhaling as you press.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back to shoulder level, then reverse the curl motion to return the weights to your sides, inhaling on the descent.
Maintain a neutral spine and avoid overarching the lower back—keep the core engaged and perform each rep in a smooth, controlled manner.
exercises.description: A standing combination move that pairs a hammer curl with an overhead dumbbell press to work the shoulder muscles while also recruiting the biceps and forearms. Perform a neutral-grip curl to shoulder height, then continue the motion into an overhead press and lower under control.

exercises.name: Wide Hand Push-up
exercises.notes: Start prone on the floor and place your hands wider than shoulder-width (about 1.
instructions: Start prone on the floor and place your hands wider than shoulder-width (about 1.5x shoulder width) with fingers splayed for stability; extend your legs and balance on your toes so your body forms a straight line from head to heels (high-plank position).
Brace your core and glutes to keep hips level and maintain a neutral neck (look slightly forward or down).
Inhale, then begin lowering by bending the elbows outward (elbows will flare more than in a close-grip push-up) while keeping the shoulders down and away from the ears.
Lower until your chest is close to the floor or your elbows reach about 90 degrees, keeping the body rigid and avoiding hip sag or excessive lumbar arch.
Pause briefly at the bottom with tension in the pectorals and serratus anterior, then exhale and press through the palms to straighten the arms and return to the starting high-plank position.
Keep controlled tempo, maintain full-body tension, and repeat for the desired repetitions. To reduce difficulty, perform the same movement from the knees with hands placed wide.
exercises.description: A prone bodyweight push-up variation with the hands placed wider than shoulder width to increase chest (pectorals) activation. From a high-plank position you lower the torso toward the floor and push back up while maintaining a straight body line.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bent over Y Raise
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips until your torso is roughly 30–45 degrees above parallel (bent-over position) with a slight bend in the knees; keep a neutral spine and neck (look at a point on the floor a few feet ahead).
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart holding a light dumbbell in each hand, palms facing each other (neutral grip).

Hinge at the hips until your torso is roughly 30–45 degrees above parallel (bent-over position) with a slight bend in the knees; keep a neutral spine and neck (look at a point on the floor a few feet ahead).
Let the arms hang straight down from the shoulders with elbows soft (slight bend) and shoulders relaxed away from the ears.

Initiate the movement by externally rotating the shoulders and lifting the dumbbells overhead and outward into a Y shape (hands move diagonally away from the body at about a 30–45° angle from the torso) until arms are roughly in line with the torso or slightly above shoulder level.
Squeeze the shoulder blades together at the top without shrugging; keep the core braced to prevent low-back extension.
Lower the dumbbells slowly and under control back to the starting hanging position, maintaining the hip hinge and neutral spine.
Repeat for the desired reps; choose light weights to maintain strict form and avoid momentum or excessive back arching.
exercises.description: A bent-over Y raise is a standing hip-hinged exercise that lifts light dumbbells in a Y shape to train the rear and lateral delts while improving upper-back stability and scapular control. It emphasizes controlled scapular retraction and external rotation through the shoulder joint.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Cable Rope Seated Row
exercises.notes: Grasp the rope attachment with a neutral grip (palms facing each other) and sit upright with a neutral spine and chest up; start with arms fully extended.
instructions: Sit on a seated row bench or platform with feet braced against the footplate and knees slightly bent.
Grasp the rope attachment with a neutral grip (palms facing each other) and sit upright with a neutral spine and chest up; start with arms fully extended.
Initiate the movement by pulling your shoulder blades back and down (scapular retraction) and drive your elbows straight back toward your torso.
Pull the rope toward your upper abdomen or lower chest, keeping elbows close to the body and leading with the elbows rather than the hands.
Pause briefly at peak contraction while squeezing the lats and upper back, maintaining an upright torso (avoid excessive trunk lean or momentum).
Slowly extend the arms back to the starting position with control, returning the shoulders to a slightly protracted position while keeping the spine neutral.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, ensuring wrists remain neutral and movements are smooth and controlled.
exercises.description: Seated cable rope row performed from a bench using a rope attachment to pull the handle toward the torso, emphasizing scapular retraction and elbow drive to work the lats and upper back. Keeps a neutral spine and controlled tempo to isolate the pulling muscles.

exercises.name: Cable Single Arm High Row
exercises.notes: Set a single handle on a high cable pulley.
instructions: Set a single handle on a high cable pulley. Adjust a bench or chest pad so your chest is firmly supported and you can sit upright without leaning forward or rotating.
Sit with feet flat on the floor, chest pressed into the support, and grab the handle with a neutral or overhand grip using one arm; start with the arm extended and shoulder slightly protracted.
Brace your core, keep your torso stationary against the chest support, and retract your shoulder blade slightly before initiating the pull.
Pull the handle down and back in a smooth arc by driving your elbow up and back toward your ribcage, focusing on squeezing the upper back and rear delt at the end range.
Pause briefly at the peak contraction while maintaining a neutral wrist and no torso rotation or shrugging, then slowly extend the arm back to the start position under control.
Repeat for the desired reps, then switch sides. Maintain steady breathing (exhale during the pull, inhale during the return) and avoid using momentum.
exercises.description: A single-arm high cable row performed with chest support to isolate the upper back while minimizing torso movement. The movement pulls a high-set handle down and back with the elbow driving toward the ribcage to emphasize the upper back and rear delts.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows and thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Retract and depress the shoulder blades (pinch the shoulder blades together) to create a stable chest platform.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over the chest so it is balanced and steady.
Inhale, lower the bar under control to the mid-chest (sternum) keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest while maintaining tightness through the core and legs.
Exhale and drive the bar upward by extending the elbows, pressing the bar back to the start position while keeping wrists neutral.
Avoid excessive elbow flare or bouncing the bar off the chest; maintain a controlled tempo and keep feet planted.
After the set, carefully re-rack the bar by guiding it back onto the bench supports before releasing your grip.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a supine pressing exercise performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also working the triceps and anterior delts. The lifter lowers a barbell to the chest and presses it back to full arm extension under control.
